title: The Tripwire Patent
text:
Picture an explosion in a parking garage. The people nearby do exactly what you would hope. Two of them drag a man clear of the smoke, one starts CPR, the rest run for safety. Nobody calls 911, because everyone who saw it has their hands full or is getting out. The call comes minutes later, from a woman who dialed once she felt safe. Those are long minutes in an emergency.
The garage had cameras. They recorded everything from the first frame, and they did nothing with it, because a camera has no way of knowing that what it just saw is worth summoning a fire brigade. The usual fix is to pre-program alarm systems to detect specific events, and that works only for the events someone thought to predefine. The ones nobody imagined in advance are exactly the ones that slip through.
This is the problem my patent goes after. It was granted this May as US 12,634,398 B2 (grant PDF), and the invention won Motorola’s Top Invention of the Year award for 2021.

title: The Tripwire Patent
section: Let the call teach the sensors
text:
Let the call teach the sensors
The idea is to use the 911 call, when it finally arrives, to teach the nearby sensors what mattered.
When the call comes in, the system finds the sensors that could have seen the incident and pulls what they recorded around that time and place, including the minutes before. Software then distills that recording into something small and checkable. The concrete version in our filing: image recognition reduces the incident video to a set of words, {explosion, fire, car, people, scream}.
From that summary the system arms what we called an alarm-escalating trigger. It has two parts. A condition, and it lives entirely in the metadata: new footage keeps getting tagged the same way, and the condition is that a new clip’s tags share at least four of those five words. And a response staged in advance for that kind of incident: notify the responders’ radios, alert the 911 call taker, open the emergency doors, sound the sirens.



The mechanism, with the example from our filing.


Then the sensors watch, in tag space rather than pixel space. The camera never compares images to images; it compares small sets of words. If a later clip’s tags meet the condition, the staged response fires. No one has to call.



The payoff, in time saved.


That second timeline is the whole point. The first incident pays the cost of human judgement, one real 911 call, and converts it into a machine-checkable description of what an emergency looks like at this place. That is the payoff: the next time, a proper response can start before any notification from a witness, or without one at all.

title: The Tripwire Patent
text:
Why I find it elegant
A tripwire armed this way is narrow on purpose. It is tied to a place, to a kind of incident, and to sensor readings that accompanied a real emergency. The camera is never asked to decide what an emergency is in general, which is the version of the problem nobody knows how to solve. It is asked whether what it sees now resembles the thing a human already declared an emergency, and that is just a similarity check.
The wire also does not have to stay where it was laid. The trigger can be copied to similar sensors in similar buildings, an office tower, a plant with the same smart-building sensors, so a lesson learned once arms tripwires in many places. Our filing sketches the far end of this too: the accumulated incidents and their sensor readings become training data for a model that learns which readings deserve a response.



One incident arms many tripwires.


This was joint work with my co-inventors Emmy Beltre, Kaveh Malakuti and Mariya Bondareva at Motorola Solutions. Filed September 2023, granted May 2026. The word set, the response actions and the garage-style scenario above are the examples from the patent itself.

title: The Poses of Normal
text:
Watch a workplace kitchen through a camera for a week and the human body only makes a few shapes in it. People stand at the counter, sit at the tables, walk in and out. The angles of the arms and legs repeat, over and over, because the room asks for the same handful of postures. Then one afternoon someone is on the floor, and that shape has never appeared before.
That is the whole idea of this patent. Instead of teaching a camera the long list of things that can go wrong, teach it the short list of shapes a body normally makes in a given room, and let anything far from that list raise its hand. My patent with Teresa Dankel, Patrick Henson and Aral Sarrafi, US 11,386,713 B2 (grant PDF), does that with pose.

title: The Poses of Normal
section: A body as a handful of numbers
text:
A body as a handful of numbers
The first step is to stop looking at pixels and start looking at posture. A neural network finds each person in view and lays a skeleton over them, lines that follow the arms bending at the elbow, the legs bending at the knee, the spine, the head. The coordinates of those joints, gathered up, are the person’s pose vector: a compact numerical description of the shape they are making, and nothing else. Not who they are, not what they are wearing, just the geometry of the body.


  

A person, their skeleton, and the pose vector it becomes.

Working in pose space instead of pixel space is what makes the rest cheap. Two numbers per joint is a tiny thing to store and compare, it runs on the camera itself, and it sidesteps a privacy problem: the system reasons about how a body is arranged without needing to recognize whose body it is.

title: The Poses of Normal
section: Learning the shapes a room asks for
text:
Learning the shapes a room asks for
Point the camera at the room and let it collect poses. Cluster them, and the clusters are the room’s normal postures: a knot of standing poses, a knot of sitting poses, a knot of walking poses, each with a center and a radius that says how much variation still counts as that posture.
One detail in the filing I am still fond of. A cluster only becomes part of normal if enough poses landed in it. A shape that showed up once or twice during training forms a tiny cluster, and tiny clusters are thrown away before the system goes live. The rare posture does not get quietly baptized as normal just because the camera happened to see it once.


  

The postures a room repeats form clusters; a fall falls outside all of them.

title: The Poses of Normal
section: Near means fine, far means look
text:
Near means fine, far means look
Once it is running, every new pose is a point measured against those clusters. Close to a cluster center, within its radius, and the pose is just another instance of a posture the room already knows, so it gets folded in and the center shifts a little to account for it. The normal keeps learning. But when a pose sits beyond the radius of every cluster, far from standing, sitting and walking alike, it is a shape the room has not made before, and the system raises an alert: a flashing box around the person, a message to a phone or a workstation, a video clip of the moment.
The distance threshold is a dial, not a fixed line. Set it at the cluster radius and you get one sensitivity; widen it to 1.2 times the radius and fewer poses trip it; tighten it to half and more do. The filing also gates the clusters by time, so a posture normal for the middle of a workday is not automatically normal at three in the morning, and describes a second scoring path that treats each cluster as a probability distribution and asks how likely the pose is under all of them at once.
The reason this one matters more to me than its security framing suggests is the shape it is best at catching. A person who has fallen, collapsed, or is lying where people do not lie makes a pose unlike any a healthy room produces. The same math that flags an intruder’s crouch flags a medical emergency, and the camera that was watching for one can call for help with the other.
This was joint work with my co-inventors Teresa Dankel, Patrick Henson and Aral Sarrafi at Motorola Solutions. Filed May 2020, granted July 2022. It shares both its inventors and its instinct with the tripwire patent that came later: learn what normal looks like here, then watch for the departure.

title: Learning Normal
text:
A guard in front of a wall of monitors is paid to notice the one moment in a week when something odd crosses a lens, and that moment looks a great deal like every uneventful hour around it. The two examples in our filing are that kind of moment: a person running through a crowded park, a speeding vehicle on a highway. Both are easy to miss live and obvious in hindsight.
The usual fix is to write rules. Draw a line on the image and alert when something crosses it, alert when a car appears where cars should not be. Rules work until the scene changes, and someone has to imagine every rule in advance. That is the same trap I wrote about in the tripwire patent: the events nobody predefined are exactly the ones that slip through.
My patent, US 11,302,117 B2 (grant PDF), takes the opposite route: it does not try to define unusual at all. The camera learns what usual looks like, everywhere in its view, and unusual is whatever the scene has not been doing.

title: Learning Normal
text:
Cell by cell
The scene is divided into a grid of cells, and statistics are kept per class of object, in effect one grid for people and another for vehicles. Each cell learns how many objects are typically in it, how fast they move, in which directions. The input is not the video itself but the metadata the analytics already produce, the class, position and velocity of each tracked object. We built detection to run on the metadata alone, without touching the images again. That makes it cheap enough to run everywhere at once, and it works the same on a live feed and on video recorded years ago.


  

One metric, two cells. The road cell's count climbs, the corner cell's stays at zero, and the anomaly is a zero that just became one. The same bookkeeping works for speed and direction.

Statistics this local catch what one model for the whole scene would average away. A car at forty kilometers an hour is background to the road cells and an alarm two cells over, where the vehicle statistics have never recorded anything at all. Fifty people spread across a plaza is an ordinary afternoon to the person grid; the same fifty packed into one corner cell is a crowd forming. And nobody tells the system which cells are road and which are sidewalk. The statistics diverge on their own, and our filing dwells on this: clusters of small person-dense cells reveal the sidewalk, high consistent speeds reveal the road, aligned trajectories reveal the paths. The geography of the scene falls out of the models.

title: Learning Normal
text:
Normal depends on the clock
A loading dock at 2 pm and the same dock at 2 am are different places. So each cell keeps separate statistics for separate stretches of the week, Monday 8 to 9 am, Wednesday 5 to 6 pm, and a moment is judged against the statistics for its own stretch.


  

The same cell, three different normals: rush hour, the middle of the night, a weekend morning.

Stretches whose statistics turn out alike are then merged into shared patterns, weekday mornings, nights, weekends. Normal is always normal for here and for now: a person at the dock on Tuesday afternoon is background, the same person on Sunday at 3 am stands out.


  

One cell's week. Similar intervals merge into shared patterns, so each moment is judged against its own kind of time.

title: Learning Normal
text:
Where it went
This one did not stay on paper. It shipped in Avigilon’s H5A cameras as Unusual Activity Detection: object-aware in the same sense as the filing, watching for people and vehicles at unusual speeds or in unusual places, and searchable after the fact, an operator can filter a day of footage down to just the moments that broke the pattern. The models take correction too, dismiss a false alarm and the statistics behind it adjust. It is also my most built-upon patent: forty patent families cite it so far.


  

Avigilon's own demo of Unusual Activity Detection in ACC 7 (streamed from Avigilon's support library). Unmute for the narration.


This was joint work with Peter Venetianer at Avigilon. Provisional filed April 2019, granted April 2022. Looking back, the conviction that carried into the tripwire patent years later started here: the metadata is enough, and learned normal beats predefined rules.

title: Anatomy of Learning, and Downsides of Being a Second-Hand Thinker
text:
Growing up, I had a constant need of understanding how objects worked, I was constantly curious, was good at math in my peer group and dug my heels in to understand the core elements of any topic.
There though when I hit a wall for long time to learn a more complex topic, I had to make concessions on asking about the why, and accepting what was told as truth, that's just how it works and proceeding to use this assumed bedrock to then build the concepts atop.
This particularly happened to keep the pace with the fire hose of syllabus coming towards me, the need to keep with the pace of learning the course for the tests and an incomplete and immature system to digest the syllabus with a pin or a bookmark.
Now as I am older and am learning purely for the sake of understanding and out of curiosity for my selfish wants. I want to learn the concepts to their depth. I am not bound by a deadline on within which I have to learn, rather am bound by my limited time — limitations bring out efficiency. I hope to make use of my limited time well by making this an efficient use of time.

title: Anatomy of Learning, and Downsides of Being a Second-Hand Thinker
text:
Downside of Second-Hand Thinking

Be a source, not a relay. — Grant Sanderson

By not understanding a topic completely and relying on another person’s explanation, I will not understand the topic at hand. I have not played with it enough to find the nook and cranny of the thought, the way the concept grows and expands, the beauty of it or where its weaknesses lie and where it would collapse. All these aspects are fun to understand, and give a different perspective to the topic at hand as well as a different view of the world.

title: About Me
text:
I am Dharanish Kedarisetti, based in Boston. Applied ML engineer with 5+ years building production models and edge systems — currently a Research Software Engineer at Amazon working on smart cart technology.
Interests: Machine Learning, Climbing, Meditation, and Photography.

title: About Me
text:
Experience
Amazon — Research Software Engineer II, Boston, MA (Oct 2022–present) Smart cart technology (Dash Carts). Rust software for 1k–5k edge devices on AWS, shopping receipt diagnosis, CI/CD pipelines, Qualcomm NNP integration, and custom camera driver development.
Motorola Solutions — Sr. Machine Learning Engineer, Somerville, MA (Jan 2020–Sept 2022) Smart camera features for context similarity, unusual activity detection, and crowd analysis. CNN model deployment via ONNX, Docker, and Kubernetes. Contributed to the patent committee.
Avigilon — Research Engineer / Software Engineer, Somerville, MA (Feb 2018–Jan 2020) Video analytics framework development focused on unusual activity detection in C++ and Python.

title: About Me
text:
Education
Northeastern University, Boston, MA — M.Sc., Telecommunication Systems Management, 2017
BITS Pilani, India — M.S., Physics (Honors) & B.E., Electronics & Instrumentation (Honors), 2013

title: About Me
text:
Patents

US11303877B2 — Method and System for Enhancing Use of Two-Dimensional Video Analytics by Using Depth Data (Avigilon Corp, granted 2022). Improves 2D video analytics by fusing depth data to validate object classifications, correcting for shadows, reflections, and distance-based size distortions. With Pietro Russo, Peter L. Venetianer, Mahesh Saptharishi.
US11386713B2 — Anomalous Pose Detection Method and System (Motorola Solutions, granted 2022). Uses neural networks to generate skeletal pose vectors and flags deviations from learned normal-behavior clusters in real time. With Teresa Dankel, Patrick Henson, Aral Sarrafi. Explained on the blog.
WO2020210504A1 / US11302117B2 — Anomaly Detection Method, System and Computer Readable Medium (Avigilon Corp / Motorola Solutions, granted 2022). Detects unusual behavior in surveillance video by modeling statistical baselines from object metadata (class, location, velocity) per scene cell — without processing raw frames. Shipped as Avigilon Unusual Activity Detection. With Peter Venetianer. Explained on the blog.
WO2020051704A1 — System and Method for Improving Speed of Similarity Based Searches (Avigilon Corp, 2020). Accelerates appearance-search queries by k-medoid clustering of selected feature vectors before database search, reducing compute without sacrificing result quality. With Nicholas John Alcock, Peter L. Venetianer.
US12634398B2 (grant PDF) — Method of Generating a Trigger Initiating an Emergency Incident Response Workflow (Motorola Solutions, granted 2026). Bootstraps smart sensors into emergency response: a 911-reported incident anchors an alarm-escalating trigger armed from sensor metadata at that time and location, and the matched response workflow launches automatically when later sensor data meets the armed condition. With Emmy Beltre, Kaveh Malakuti, Mariya Bondareva. Motorola Solutions Top Invention of the Year, 2021. Explained on the blog.

title: About Me
text:
Papers

Large-scale Indoor Mapping with Failure Detection and Recovery in SLAM — Sharmin Rahman, Rob DiPietro, Dharanish Kedarisetti, Vinod Kulathumani. IROS 2024. Proposes an automated visual-inertial SLAM service with a health tracker for faulty measurements and continuous session merging, validated in grocery stores spanning 1700–3700 m². [PDF] [Amazon Science]
Plus Disease in Retinopathy of Prematurity: Improving Diagnosis by Ranking Disease Severity and Using Quantitative Image Analysis — Jayashree Kalpathy-Cramer, J Peter Campbell, Deniz Erdogmus, Peng Tian, Dharanish Kedarisetti, et al. Ophthalmology 123(11):2345–2351, 2016. Shows that while experts disagree on categorical ROP diagnosis, pairwise severity ranking yields good inter-expert correlation (mean r = 0.84), and that computer-based image analysis can model this continuous spectrum. [DOI] [PubMed]
